phases.summary: Colombia start on the front foot, pressing aggressively and repeatedly creating chances through wide service from the left and shots from distance. DR Congo respond by using early left-channel transitions and direct attacking outlets, with goalkeeper Lionel Mpasi already forced into key interventions.

formations_blocks: Colombia sustained high pressure / attacking dominance
Colombia pressing and winning the ball back in midfield
adjustments: DR Congo deliberately slowing the game down after absorbing pressure
Colombia's early fluidity fading as both teams begin breaking down in the final third
key_matchups: Lionel Mpasi vs Johan Mojica
Lionel Mpasi vs Luis Díaz
Lionel Mpasi vs Gustavo Puerta
Arthur Masuaku vs Daniel Muñoz
Yoane Wissa pressing Camilo Vargas
phases.summary: Colombia dominated the first half of this phase, pinning DR Congo back and generating repeated shots from distance and inside the box, all repeatedly denied by Lionel Mpasi. DR Congo spent long spells in a deep block, looking to slow the tempo and survive the pressure, before briefly threatening through Wissa’s press on Vargas. By the end of the phase, Colombia’s early rhythm had faded and both sides were struggling to progress cleanly into the final third.

formations_blocks: Colombia working the ball around the final third
DR Congo stuck in their own half
Colombia chasing a breakthrough against a resolute DR Congo defence
adjustments: DR Congo substitution: Ngal’ayel Mukau off, Noah Sadiki on
Colombia and DR Congo both remained scoreless through the half with no structural change explicitly described
key_matchups: Cédric Bakambu vs Colombia keeper Vargas
Colombia attackers vs Lionel Mpasi
phases.summary: Colombia dominated territory and volume, spending plenty of time in the final third and registering far more penalty-area touches and shots, though most efforts came from distance. DR Congo defended stubbornly, stayed largely pinned in their own half, and relied on a brief late chance through Bakambu before the break and a halftime midfield change.

formations_blocks: DR Congo defensive line/shape holding firm under pressure
Colombia in sustained attacking possession around a compact DR Congo block
adjustments: Colombia introduced Juan Quintero for James Rodríguez to add creativity between the lines
Colombia introduced Jhon Córdoba for Luis Suárez to change the centre-forward profile
DR Congo introduced Simon Banza for Cédric Bakambu to refresh the attack
Luis Díaz kept drifting/isolating on the left and inside channels to create 1v1s
Colombia used side-to-side circulation and overlaps/inside combinations to open the box

Juan Quintero linking play into Muñoz on the right
Jhon Arias arriving for the follow-up chance after Mpasi's save
Wissa and Axel Tuanzebe on DR Congo's transition break
phases.summary: The phase was defined by Colombia’s increasing territorial control and repeated attempts to break down a disciplined DR Congo defensive structure, with Díaz repeatedly central to the danger. DR Congo responded with compact defending and direct breaks, while both sides used substitutions to alter the attacking dynamic: Colombia added Quintero and Córdoba for more creativity and presence, and DR Congo brought on Banza to sharpen their forward play.

DR Congo resorting to long balls out of defence
DR Congo attacking with width in the forward half
Colombia defending and clearing through compact positioning under late pressure
adjustments: DR Congo substitutions: Charles Pickel and Nathanaël Mbuku on, with Samuel Moutoussamy and Edo Kayembe off
DR Congo shifted toward more direct play after the goal
Aaron Wan-Bissaka providing deliveries from throw-ins in the final third
Colombia repeatedly exploiting offside-line runs and quick attacks through midfield

Aaron Wan-Bissaka vs Colombia’s aerial clearances
phases.summary: Colombia’s 76th-minute breakthrough came from a through-ball and a scramble that opened space for Daniel Muñoz to finish, after which they kept hunting a second goal with aggressive forward pressure. DR Congo responded by going more direct and then more expansive late on, but Colombia’s defensive structure and clearing work held up, while Mpasi kept the margin down with another save.
